require "digest"

module ReviewPlatforms
  class TrustpilotAdapter
    PARSER_VERSION = "trustpilot-html-v1"
    REVIEW_CARD_SELECTORS = [
      "[data-service-review-card-paper]",
      "article[data-testid='review-card']",
      "article[data-review-id]",
      "[data-review-id]"
    ].freeze

    def valid_url?(url)
      Product.source_identity(url).present?
    end

    def external_id(url)
      Product.source_identity(url)&.fetch(:external_id)
    end

    def canonical_url(url)
      identity = Product.source_identity(url)
      return unless identity

      "https://www.trustpilot.com/review/#{identity[:external_id]}"
    end

    def fetch_pages(_url)
      raise NotImplementedError, "Network fetching belongs to the ingestion fetcher"
    end

    def parse_reviews(page_html, source_url:)
      document = Nokogiri::HTML(page_html)

      review_cards(document).filter_map do |card|
        parsed_review(card, source_url:)
      end
    end

    def parse_product_metadata(page_html)
      document = Nokogiri::HTML(page_html)

      { name: product_name(document) }.compact
    end

    private
      def product_name(document)
        heading = normalize_text(document.at_css("h1")&.text)
        title = normalize_text(document.at_css("title")&.text)

        normalize_product_name(heading || title)
      end

      def normalize_product_name(text)
        normalize_text(text)
          &.sub(/\s+\|\s+Trustpilot\z/, "")
          &.sub(/\s+Reviews\z/, "")
          &.presence
      end

      def review_cards(document)
        REVIEW_CARD_SELECTORS.flat_map { |selector| document.css(selector).to_a }.uniq
      end

      def parsed_review(card, source_url:)
        body = text_at(card, [
          "[data-service-review-text-typography]",
          "[itemprop='reviewBody']",
          "p"
        ])
        title = text_at(card, [
          "[data-service-review-title-typography]",
          "h2",
          "h3"
        ])
        rating = extract_rating(card)
        reviewer_label = text_at(card, [
          "[data-consumer-name-typography]",
          "[data-testid='consumer-name']",
          "[itemprop='author']",
          "aside a",
          "aside span"
        ])
        external_review_id = extract_external_review_id(card)
        normalized_body = normalize_text(body)

        return if normalized_body.blank?

        {
          external_review_id: external_review_id,
          content_hash: content_hash(normalized_body, rating:, reviewer_label:),
          source_url: review_source_url(card, source_url),
          rating: rating,
          title: normalize_text(title),
          body: normalized_body,
          reviewer_label: normalize_text(reviewer_label),
          review_date: extract_review_date(card),
          raw_payload: {
            parser: PARSER_VERSION,
            external_review_id: external_review_id,
            rating: rating,
            title: normalize_text(title),
            body: normalized_body,
            reviewer_label: normalize_text(reviewer_label)
          }.compact
        }.compact
      end

      def text_at(node, selectors)
        selectors.each do |selector|
          text = normalize_text(node.at_css(selector)&.text)
          return text if text.present?
        end

        nil
      end

      def normalize_text(text)
        text.to_s.squish.presence
      end

      def extract_rating(card)
        raw_rating =
          card["data-service-review-rating"] ||
          card["data-rating"] ||
          card.at_css("[data-service-review-rating]")&.[]("data-service-review-rating") ||
          card.at_css("[itemprop='ratingValue']")&.[]("content") ||
          card.at_css("img[alt*='Rated']")&.[]("alt")

        raw_rating.to_s[/\d+/]&.to_i
      end

      def extract_external_review_id(card)
        raw_id =
          card["data-service-review-id"] ||
          card["data-review-id"] ||
          card.at_css("a[href*='/reviews/']")&.[]("href")&.split("/reviews/")&.last

        normalize_text(raw_id)&.split(/[?#]/)&.first
      end

      def extract_review_date(card)
        raw_date =
          card.at_css("time")&.[]("datetime") ||
          card.at_css("[data-service-review-date-time-ago]")&.[]("datetime")

        Time.zone.parse(raw_date) if raw_date.present?
      rescue ArgumentError
        nil
      end

      def review_source_url(card, source_url)
        review_path = card.at_css("a[href*='/reviews/']")&.[]("href")
        return source_url if review_path.blank?

        URI.join(source_url, review_path).to_s
      rescue URI::InvalidURIError
        source_url
      end

      def content_hash(body, rating:, reviewer_label:)
        Digest::SHA256.hexdigest([ body, rating, normalize_text(reviewer_label) ].compact.join("\n"))
      end
  end
end

require "json"
require "nokogiri"

module ReviewPlatforms
  class TrustpilotProbe
    MINIMUM_USABLE_REVIEWS = 20
    STRONG_USABLE_REVIEWS = 50
    REVIEW_TEXT_SELECTORS = [
      "[data-service-review-text-typography]",
      "[data-service-review-text]",
      "[itemprop='reviewBody']",
      "p[data-service-review-text-typography]",
      "article p"
    ].freeze
    BLOCK_PATTERNS = [
      /captcha/i,
      /access denied/i,
      /verify you are human/i,
      /unusual traffic/i,
      /security check/i
    ].freeze

    def initialize(html:, source_url:, fetch_metadata: {})
      @html = html.to_s
      @source_url = source_url
      @fetch_metadata = fetch_metadata
      @document = Nokogiri::HTML(@html)
    end

    def call
      usable_raw_review_count = review_bodies.count
      captcha_or_block_detected = blocked?
      corpus_quality = classify(usable_raw_review_count, captcha_or_block_detected)

      {
        status: captcha_or_block_detected ? "blocked" : "ok",
        source_url: @source_url,
        html_bytes: @html.bytesize,
        title: title,
        title_detected: title.present?,
        trust_score: trust_score,
        trust_score_detected: trust_score.present?,
        rating_distribution_detected: rating_distribution_detected?,
        review_count: review_count,
        review_count_detected: review_count.present?,
        usable_raw_review_count:,
        minimum_usable_reviews: MINIMUM_USABLE_REVIEWS,
        strong_usable_reviews: STRONG_USABLE_REVIEWS,
        trustpilot_ai_summary_detected: trustpilot_ai_summary_detected?,
        captcha_or_block_detected:,
        corpus_quality:,
        recommended_adapter: recommended_adapter(corpus_quality),
        fetch_metadata: @fetch_metadata
      }
    end

    private

    def title
      @title ||= @document.at_css("title")&.text.to_s.squish.presence
    end

    def trust_score
      @trust_score ||= json_ld_aggregate_rating["ratingValue"]&.to_s ||
        @html[/TrustScore\s*([0-9.]+)/i, 1]
    end

    def review_count
      @review_count ||= begin
        raw_count = json_ld_aggregate_rating["reviewCount"] ||
          @html[/([0-9][0-9,.\s]*)\s+reviews/i, 1]
        normalize_count(raw_count)
      end
    end

    def rating_distribution_detected?
      @rating_distribution_detected ||= begin
        distribution_text = @document.text

        %w[5-star 4-star 3-star 2-star 1-star].all? do |label|
          distribution_text.match?(/#{Regexp.escape(label)}/i) || distribution_text.match?(/#{label.first}\s+star/i)
        end
      end
    end

    def trustpilot_ai_summary_detected?
      @trustpilot_ai_summary_detected ||= @html.match?(/AI[- ]generated summary|AI[- ]created summary|Trustpilot AI/i)
    end

    def blocked?
      BLOCK_PATTERNS.any? { |pattern| @html.match?(pattern) }
    end

    def review_bodies
      @review_bodies ||= REVIEW_TEXT_SELECTORS.flat_map do |selector|
        @document.css(selector).map { |node| node.text.squish }
      end.select { |body| usable_review_body?(body) }.uniq
    end

    def usable_review_body?(body)
      body.length >= 40 &&
        body.split.size >= 8 &&
        !body.match?(/Trustpilot AI|AI[- ]generated summary|AI[- ]created summary/i)
    end

    def classify(usable_raw_review_count, captcha_or_block_detected)
      return "fail" if captcha_or_block_detected || usable_raw_review_count.zero?
      return "strong" if usable_raw_review_count >= STRONG_USABLE_REVIEWS
      return "viable" if usable_raw_review_count >= MINIMUM_USABLE_REVIEWS

      "thin"
    end

    def recommended_adapter(corpus_quality)
      %w[strong viable].include?(corpus_quality) ? "trustpilot" : "manual_import"
    end

    def json_ld_aggregate_rating
      @json_ld_aggregate_rating ||= json_ld_objects.filter_map { |object| find_aggregate_rating(object) }.first || {}
    end

    def json_ld_objects
      @json_ld_objects ||= @document.css("script[type='application/ld+json']").filter_map do |script|
        JSON.parse(script.text)
      rescue JSON::ParserError
        nil
      end
    end

    def find_aggregate_rating(value)
      case value
      when Hash
        return value["aggregateRating"] if value["aggregateRating"].is_a?(Hash)

        value.each_value do |nested_value|
          rating = find_aggregate_rating(nested_value)
          return rating if rating.present?
        end
      when Array
        value.each do |nested_value|
          rating = find_aggregate_rating(nested_value)
          return rating if rating.present?
        end
      end

      nil
    end

    def normalize_count(raw_count)
      return if raw_count.blank?

      raw_count.to_s.gsub(/[^\d]/, "").presence&.to_i
    end
  end
end

#!/usr/bin/env ruby

require_relative "../config/environment"
require_relative "scrape_probe_cli"

exit Script::ScrapeProbeCli.new.call(ARGV)

require "json"
require "optparse"

module Script
  class ScrapeProbeCli
    DEFAULT_URL = "https://www.trustpilot.com/review/quickbooks.intuit.com"
    DEFAULT_FIXTURE_PATH = Rails.root.join("test/fixtures/files/trustpilot_quickbooks.html")

    def initialize(fetcher: Ingestion::Fetcher.new, probe_class: ReviewPlatforms::TrustpilotProbe, output: $stdout)
      @fetcher = fetcher
      @probe_class = probe_class
      @output = output
    end

    def call(argv)
      options = parse_options(argv)
      source_url = argv.first || DEFAULT_URL
      result = options[:fixture_path] ? probe_fixture(options[:fixture_path], source_url) : probe_live(source_url, options)

      @output.puts(JSON.pretty_generate(result[:report]))
      result[:exit_status]
    end

    private

    def parse_options(argv)
      options = {
        save_fixture: false,
        save_fixture_path: DEFAULT_FIXTURE_PATH
      }

      parser = OptionParser.new do |opts|
        opts.banner = "Usage: script/scrape_probe.rb [URL] [--fixture PATH] [--save-fixture [PATH]]"
        opts.separator ""
        opts.separator "Examples:"
        opts.separator "  script/scrape_probe.rb --fixture test/fixtures/files/trustpilot_quickbooks.html"
        opts.separator "  script/scrape_probe.rb https://www.trustpilot.com/review/quickbooks.intuit.com --save-fixture"

        opts.on("--fixture PATH", "Probe an existing local HTML fixture without network access") do |path|
          options[:fixture_path] = expand_path(path)
        end

        opts.on("--save-fixture [PATH]", "Save fetched HTML to PATH, default: #{DEFAULT_FIXTURE_PATH}") do |path|
          options[:save_fixture] = true
          options[:save_fixture_path] = expand_path(path) if path.present?
        end
      end

      parser.parse!(argv)
      options
    end

    def probe_fixture(fixture_path, source_url)
      html = File.read(fixture_path)
      metadata = {
        input_mode: "fixture",
        fixture_path: fixture_path,
        html_bytes: html.bytesize
      }

      report = report_for(html: html, source_url: source_url, fetch_metadata: metadata).merge(input_mode: "fixture")
      { report: report, exit_status: exit_status_for(report) }
    rescue Errno::ENOENT
      report = failure_report(
        source_url: source_url,
        status: "fixture_missing",
        error_code: "fixture_missing",
        fetch_metadata: { input_mode: "fixture", fixture_path: fixture_path }
      )
      { report: report, exit_status: 1 }
    end

    def probe_live(source_url, options)
      fetch_result = @fetcher.fetch(source_url)

      unless fetch_result.success?
        report = failure_report(
          source_url: source_url,
          status: "fetch_failed",
          error_code: fetch_result.error_code,
          fetch_metadata: fetch_result.metadata
        )
        return { report: report, exit_status: 1 }
      end

      report = report_for(html: fetch_result.body, source_url: source_url, fetch_metadata: fetch_result.metadata)

      if options[:save_fixture]
        File.write(options[:save_fixture_path], fetch_result.body)
        report[:saved_fixture] = options[:save_fixture_path].to_s
      end

      { report: report, exit_status: exit_status_for(report) }
    end

    def report_for(html:, source_url:, fetch_metadata:)
      probe_result = @probe_class.new(html: html, source_url: source_url, fetch_metadata: fetch_metadata).call

      normalize_report(probe_result).merge(
        http_status: fetch_metadata[:http_status],
        fetch_metadata: fetch_metadata
      )
    end

    def normalize_report(result)
      {
        status: result[:status],
        source_url: result[:source_url],
        html_bytes: result[:html_bytes],
        title_detected: result[:title_detected],
        title: result[:title],
        trust_score_detected: result[:trust_score_detected],
        trust_score: result[:trust_score],
        rating_distribution_detected: result[:rating_distribution_detected],
        review_count_detected: result[:review_count_detected],
        review_count: result[:review_count],
        usable_raw_review_body_count: result[:usable_raw_review_count],
        minimum_usable_review_bodies: result[:minimum_usable_reviews],
        strong_usable_review_bodies: result[:strong_usable_reviews],
        trustpilot_ai_summary_detected: result[:trustpilot_ai_summary_detected],
        captcha_block_detected: result[:captcha_or_block_detected],
        corpus_quality: result[:corpus_quality],
        recommended_adapter: result[:recommended_adapter]
      }
    end

    def failure_report(source_url:, status:, error_code:, fetch_metadata:)
      {
        status: status,
        http_status: fetch_metadata[:http_status],
        source_url: source_url,
        html_bytes: fetch_metadata[:html_bytes],
        title_detected: false,
        title: nil,
        trust_score_detected: false,
        trust_score: nil,
        rating_distribution_detected: false,
        review_count_detected: false,
        review_count: nil,
        usable_raw_review_body_count: 0,
        minimum_usable_review_bodies: ReviewPlatforms::TrustpilotProbe::MINIMUM_USABLE_REVIEWS,
        strong_usable_review_bodies: ReviewPlatforms::TrustpilotProbe::STRONG_USABLE_REVIEWS,
        trustpilot_ai_summary_detected: false,
        captcha_block_detected: false,
        corpus_quality: "fail",
        recommended_adapter: "manual_import",
        error_code: error_code,
        fetch_metadata: fetch_metadata
      }
    end

    def exit_status_for(report)
      report[:corpus_quality] == "fail" ? 1 : 0
    end

    def expand_path(path)
      pathname = Pathname.new(path)
      pathname.absolute? ? pathname.to_s : Rails.root.join(path).to_s
    end
  end
end

require "net/http"
require "timeout"

module Ingestion
  class Fetcher
    TRUSTPILOT_HOSTS = %w[trustpilot.com www.trustpilot.com].freeze
    MAX_REDIRECTS = 2
    TIMEOUT_SECONDS = 10
    MAX_BYTES = 5.megabytes
    REDIRECT_STATUSES = [ 301, 302, 303, 307, 308 ].freeze
    BLOCKED_HTTP_STATUSES = [ 403, 429 ].freeze

    Result = Struct.new(:successful, :body, :metadata, :error_code, keyword_init: true) do
      def success?
        successful
      end
    end

    Response = Struct.new(:status, :headers, :body, keyword_init: true)

    def initialize(transport: nil)
      @transport = transport || method(:perform_request)
    end

    def fetch(source_url)
      uri = parse_uri(source_url)
      metadata = base_metadata(source_url)

      return failure("invalid_url", metadata) unless uri&.is_a?(URI::HTTP)
      return failure("unsupported_host", metadata.merge(final_url: uri.to_s)) unless allowed_host?(uri)

      redirects = 0

      loop do
        metadata[:pages_attempted] += 1
        response = @transport.call(uri, request_limits)
        status = response.status.to_i
        metadata[:http_status] = status

        if redirect_status?(status)
          redirects += 1
          metadata[:redirect_count] = redirects
          return failure("too_many_redirects", metadata.merge(final_url: uri.to_s)) if redirects > MAX_REDIRECTS

          redirect_uri = resolve_redirect(uri, header_value(response.headers, "location"))
          return failure("invalid_redirect", metadata.merge(final_url: uri.to_s)) unless redirect_uri&.is_a?(URI::HTTP)
          return failure("redirect_host_not_allowed", metadata.merge(final_url: redirect_uri.to_s)) unless allowed_host?(redirect_uri)

          uri = redirect_uri
          next
        end

        unless success_status?(status)
          return failure(http_error_code(status), metadata.merge(final_url: uri.to_s))
        end

        body = response.body.to_s
        html_bytes = body.bytesize
        return failure("response_too_large", metadata.merge(final_url: uri.to_s, html_bytes:, max_bytes: MAX_BYTES)) if html_bytes > MAX_BYTES

        metadata[:pages_succeeded] += 1
        metadata[:final_url] = uri.to_s
        metadata[:html_bytes] = html_bytes
        metadata[:content_type] = header_value(response.headers, "content-type")

        return Result.new(successful: true, body:, metadata:, error_code: nil)
      end
    rescue Timeout::Error, Net::OpenTimeout, Net::ReadTimeout
      failure("timeout", metadata)
    rescue ResponseTooLarge
      failure("response_too_large", metadata.merge(final_url: uri&.to_s, max_bytes: MAX_BYTES))
    rescue SocketError, SystemCallError
      failure("network_error", metadata)
    end

    private

    def perform_request(uri, limits)
      request = Net::HTTP::Get.new(uri)
      request["User-Agent"] = "ReviewLensAI/1.0"
      request["Accept"] = "text/html,application/xhtml+xml"

      body = +""
      response_headers = {}
      response_status = nil

      Net::HTTP.start(
        uri.host,
        uri.port,
        use_ssl: uri.scheme == "https",
        open_timeout: limits[:timeout_seconds],
        read_timeout: limits[:timeout_seconds]
      ) do |http|
        http.request(request) do |response|
          response_status = response.code.to_i
          response.each_header { |key, value| response_headers[key] = value }
          response.read_body do |chunk|
            body << chunk
            raise ResponseTooLarge if body.bytesize > limits[:max_bytes]
          end
        end
      end

      Response.new(status: response_status, headers: response_headers, body:)
    end

    def request_limits
      { timeout_seconds: TIMEOUT_SECONDS, max_bytes: MAX_BYTES }
    end

    def base_metadata(source_url)
      {
        source_url:,
        pages_attempted: 0,
        pages_succeeded: 0,
        redirect_count: 0,
        timeout_seconds: TIMEOUT_SECONDS,
        max_bytes: MAX_BYTES
      }
    end

    def parse_uri(source_url)
      URI.parse(source_url.to_s.strip)
    rescue URI::InvalidURIError
      nil
    end

    def allowed_host?(uri)
      TRUSTPILOT_HOSTS.include?(uri.host.to_s.downcase)
    end

    def redirect_status?(status)
      REDIRECT_STATUSES.include?(status)
    end

    def success_status?(status)
      status.between?(200, 299)
    end

    def http_error_code(status)
      return "blocked" if BLOCKED_HTTP_STATUSES.include?(status)

      "http_#{status}"
    end

    def resolve_redirect(current_uri, location)
      return if location.blank?

      current_uri + location
    rescue URI::InvalidURIError
      nil
    end

    def header_value(headers, name)
      headers.find { |key, _value| key.to_s.downcase == name }&.last
    end

    def failure(error_code, metadata)
      Result.new(successful: false, body: nil, metadata:, error_code:)
    end

    class ResponseTooLarge < StandardError; end
  end
end

Whitelists `trustpilot.com`, sets `User-Agent: ReviewLensAI/1.0`, treats 403/429 as `error_code: "blocked"`. - `app/services/review_platforms/trustpilot_probe.rb` -> analyzes fetched HTML to classify corpus quality and recommend an adapter. - `app/services/review_platforms/trustpilot_adapter.rb` -> parses review cards (never reached, since fetch dies at 403). - `script/scrape_probe.rb` -> CLI runner. In the live flow, the fetcher returns `error_code: "blocked"` at the 403 and **the probe never sees the body**. The block-detection test fixture is fictional.** `test/fixtures/files/trustpilot_blocked_captcha.html` says "Verify you are human" / "CAPTCHA challenge". Trustpilot never serves that. # Review HTML Fixtures

These fixtures are hand-authored, Trustpilot-like HTML pages for parser,
probe, and fixture-classification tests. They are intentionally static and
must not require live scraping or network access.

- `trustpilot_viable_corpus.html`: reusable parser-facing corpus with 20 raw
  user review cards. Expected classification: `viable`.
- `trustpilot_thin_corpus.html`: reusable parser-facing corpus with 5 raw user
  review cards. Expected classification: `thin`.
- `trustpilot_blocked_captcha.html`: CAPTCHA-like blocked page with no review
  cards. Expected classification: `blocked`.

The corpus fixtures include Trustpilot-like selectors used by the local
adapter, plus `data-review-card="true"` markers for lightweight fixture-count
checks. Keep the text synthetic, product-realistic, and deterministic.

module ReviewFixtureClassifier
  FixtureResult = Data.define(:path, :quality, :review_count, :blocked)

  module_function

  def classify(file_name)
    path = Rails.root.join("test/fixtures/files", file_name)
    html = File.read(path)
    blocked = html.match?(/captcha|blocked|verify you are human|unusual traffic/i)
    review_count = html.scan(/data-review-card=/).count

    FixtureResult.new(
      path: path.to_s,
      quality: quality_for(review_count, blocked),
      review_count: review_count,
      blocked: blocked
    )
  end

  def quality_for(review_count, blocked)
    return :blocked if blocked
    return :viable if review_count >= 20

    :thin
  end
end

class IngestReviewsJob < ApplicationJob
  queue_as :default

  FETCH_FAILURE_MESSAGE = "Trustpilot fetch failed: %s"
  FETCH_BLOCKED_MESSAGE = "Trustpilot fetch blocked by remote host"
  NO_USABLE_REVIEWS_MESSAGE = "No usable Trustpilot review cards found. Use manual import or another public Trustpilot URL."
  SUMMARIZATION_FAILURE_MESSAGE = "Review summarization failed: %s"
  BLOCKED_WARNING = "Trustpilot returned blocking page before parsing"
  THIN_CORPUS_WARNING = "Trustpilot returned fewer than 20 usable raw reviews; manual import recommended"

  discard_on ActiveJob::DeserializationError

  def perform(ingestion_run)
    @ingestion_run = ingestion_run
    @product = ingestion_run.product
    @adapter = ReviewPlatforms::TrustpilotAdapter.new

    mark_fetching

    fetch_result = Ingestion::Fetcher.new.fetch(product.source_url)
    update_fetch_counters(fetch_result.metadata)

    return mark_failed(fetch_failure_message(fetch_result.error_code)) unless fetch_result.success?

    probe_result = probe(fetch_result)
    ingestion_run.update!(
      raw_fetch_metadata: { fetch: fetch_result.metadata, probe: probe_result },
      warnings: probe_warnings(probe_result)
    )

    return mark_failed(FETCH_BLOCKED_MESSAGE) if probe_result[:captcha_or_block_detected]

    mark_parsing

    parsed_reviews = adapter.parse_reviews(fetch_result.body, source_url: product.source_url)
    update_product_metadata(fetch_result.body)
    import_result = Ingestion::Importer.import(product, parsed_reviews)

    ingestion_run.update!(
      reviews_found: parsed_reviews.size,
      reviews_imported: import_result.imported,
      reviews_skipped: import_result.skipped
    )

    return mark_failed(NO_USABLE_REVIEWS_MESSAGE) if product.reviews.reload.none?

    mark_ready
  rescue StandardError => error
    mark_failed(error.message)
  end

  private
    attr_reader :adapter, :ingestion_run, :product

    def mark_fetching
      product.update!(ingestion_status: "fetching", ingestion_error: nil)
      ingestion_run.update!(
        status: "fetching",
        started_at: Time.current,
        parser_version: ReviewPlatforms::TrustpilotAdapter::PARSER_VERSION,
        error: nil,
        warnings: [],
        raw_fetch_metadata: {}
      )
    end

    def mark_parsing
      product.update!(ingestion_status: "parsing")
      ingestion_run.update!(status: "parsing")
    end

    def mark_ready
      product.update!(ingestion_status: "summarizing")
      ingestion_run.update!(status: "summarizing")

      Ingestion::SummaryBuilder.new(product:).call
      summarize_reviews

      product.update!(ingestion_status: "ready", ingestion_error: nil)
      ingestion_run.update!(status: "ready", finished_at: Time.current)
    end

    def summarize_reviews
      ReviewAnalysis::BatchSummarizer.call(product:)
    rescue StandardError => error
      raise StandardError, SUMMARIZATION_FAILURE_MESSAGE % error.message
    end

    def mark_failed(error_message)
      product.update!(ingestion_status: "failed", ingestion_error: error_message)
      ingestion_run.update!(status: "failed", error: error_message, finished_at: Time.current)
    end

    def update_fetch_counters(metadata)
      ingestion_run.update!(
        raw_fetch_metadata: { fetch: metadata },
        pages_attempted: metadata[:pages_attempted].to_i,
        pages_succeeded: metadata[:pages_succeeded].to_i
      )
    end

    def probe(fetch_result)
      ReviewPlatforms::TrustpilotProbe.new(
        html: fetch_result.body,
        source_url: product.source_url,
        fetch_metadata: fetch_result.metadata
      ).call
    end

    def probe_warnings(probe_result)
      [
        (BLOCKED_WARNING if probe_result[:captcha_or_block_detected]),
        (THIN_CORPUS_WARNING if probe_result[:corpus_quality] == "thin")
      ].compact
    end

    def fetch_failure_message(error_code)
      return FETCH_BLOCKED_MESSAGE if error_code == "blocked"

      FETCH_FAILURE_MESSAGE % error_code
    end

    def update_product_metadata(html)
      metadata = adapter.parse_product_metadata(html)
      return if product.name.present? || metadata[:name].blank?

      product.update!(name: metadata[:name])
    end
end

require "test_helper"

class Ingestion::FetcherTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  Response = Struct.new(:status, :headers, :body, keyword_init: true)

  test "fetches whitelisted trustpilot url records metadata" do
    fetcher = Ingestion::Fetcher.new(transport: lambda { |_uri, limits|
      assert_equal 10, limits[:timeout_seconds]
      assert_equal 5.megabytes, limits[:max_bytes]

      Response.new(status: 200, headers: { "content-type" => "text/html" }, body: "<html>ok</html>")
    })

    result = fetcher.fetch("https://www.trustpilot.com/review/quickbooks.intuit.com")

    assert_predicate result, :success?
    assert_equal "<html>ok</html>", result.body
    assert_equal 15, result.metadata[:html_bytes]
    assert_equal 1, result.metadata[:pages_attempted]
    assert_equal 1, result.metadata[:pages_succeeded]
    assert_equal 0, result.metadata[:redirect_count]
    assert_equal 200, result.metadata[:http_status]
    assert_equal "text/html", result.metadata[:content_type]
  end

  test "fails blocked http responses before parsing" do
    fetcher = Ingestion::Fetcher.new(transport: lambda { |_uri, _limits|
      Response.new(status: 403, headers: { "content-type" => "text/html" }, body: "<html>blocked</html>")
    })

    result = fetcher.fetch("https://www.trustpilot.com/review/quickbooks.intuit.com")

    assert_not result.success?
    assert_equal "blocked", result.error_code
    assert_nil result.body
    assert_equal 403, result.metadata[:http_status]
    assert_equal 1, result.metadata[:pages_attempted]
    assert_equal 0, result.metadata[:pages_succeeded]
  end

  test "fails server error http responses before parsing" do
    fetcher = Ingestion::Fetcher.new(transport: lambda { |_uri, _limits|
      Response.new(status: 500, headers: {}, body: "<html>server error</html>")
    })

    result = fetcher.fetch("https://www.trustpilot.com/review/quickbooks.intuit.com")

    assert_not result.success?
    assert_equal "http_500", result.error_code
    assert_nil result.body
    assert_equal 500, result.metadata[:http_status]
    assert_equal 1, result.metadata[:pages_attempted]
    assert_equal 0, result.metadata[:pages_succeeded]
  end

  test "rejects non trustpilot hosts before requesting" do
    requested = false
    fetcher = Ingestion::Fetcher.new(transport: lambda { |_uri, _limits|
      requested = true
      Response.new(status: 200, headers: {}, body: "should not happen")
    })

    result = fetcher.fetch("https://example.com/review/quickbooks.intuit.com")

    assert_not result.success?
    assert_equal "unsupported_host", result.error_code
    assert_not requested
  end

  test "rejects invalid url before requesting" do
    requested = false
    fetcher = Ingestion::Fetcher.new(transport: lambda { |_uri, _limits|
      requested = true
      Response.new(status: 200, headers: {}, body: "should not happen")
    })

    result = fetcher.fetch("not a url")

    assert_not result.success?
    assert_equal "invalid_url", result.error_code
    assert_not requested
  end

  test "follows at most two trustpilot redirects" do
    responses = [
      Response.new(status: 302, headers: { "location" => "https://www.trustpilot.com/review/quickbooks.intuit.com?languages=all" }, body: ""),
      Response.new(status: 301, headers: { "location" => "https://trustpilot.com/review/quickbooks.intuit.com?sort=recency" }, body: ""),
      Response.new(status: 200, headers: {}, body: "<html>redirected</html>")
    ]
    requested_urls = []
    fetcher = Ingestion::Fetcher.new(transport: lambda { |uri, _limits|
      requested_urls << uri.to_s
      responses.shift
    })

    result = fetcher.fetch("https://www.trustpilot.com/review/quickbooks.intuit.com")

    assert_predicate result, :success?
    assert_equal "<html>redirected</html>", result.body
    assert_equal 3, result.metadata[:pages_attempted]
    assert_equal 2, result.metadata[:redirect_count]
    assert_equal [
      "https://www.trustpilot.com/review/quickbooks.intuit.com",
      "https://www.trustpilot.com/review/quickbooks.intuit.com?languages=all",
      "https://trustpilot.com/review/quickbooks.intuit.com?sort=recency"
    ], requested_urls
  end

  test "fails when redirect count exceeds two" do
    fetcher = Ingestion::Fetcher.new(transport: lambda { |_uri, _limits|
      Response.new(status: 302, headers: { "location" => "/review/quickbooks.intuit.com" }, body: "")
    })

    result = fetcher.fetch("https://www.trustpilot.com/review/quickbooks.intuit.com")

    assert_not result.success?
    assert_equal "too_many_redirects", result.error_code
    assert_equal 3, result.metadata[:pages_attempted]
    assert_equal 3, result.metadata[:redirect_count]
  end

  test "fails when redirect leaves trustpilot whitelist" do
    fetcher = Ingestion::Fetcher.new(transport: lambda { |_uri, _limits|
      Response.new(status: 302, headers: { "location" => "https://evil.example/review/quickbooks.intuit.com" }, body: "")

  test "fails when response exceeds five megabytes" do
    fetcher = Ingestion::Fetcher.new(transport: lambda { |_uri, limits|
      Response.new(status: 200, headers: {}, body: "x" * (limits[:max_bytes] + 1))

  test "maps timeout transport errors" do
    fetcher = Ingestion::Fetcher.new(transport: lambda { |_uri, _limits| raise Timeout::Error })

    result = fetcher.fetch("https://www.trustpilot.com/review/quickbooks.intuit.com")

def probe(fetch_result)
      ReviewPlatforms::TrustpilotProbe.new(
        html: fetch_result.body,
        source_url: product.source_url,
        fetch_metadata: fetch_result.metadata
      ).call
    end

    def persist_probe(fetch_result)
      probe_result = probe(fetch_result)
      ingestion_run.update!(
        raw_fetch_metadata: { fetch: fetch_result.metadata, probe: probe_result },
        warnings: probe_warnings(probe_result)
      )
      probe_result
    end

    def blocked_with_body?(fetch_result)
      fetch_result.error_code == "blocked" && fetch_result.body.present?
    end

## What changed

### 1. Probe now folds `http_status` into the blocked decision
`app/services/review_platforms/trustpilot_probe.rb`
- Added `BLOCKED_HTTP_STATUSES = [403, 429].freeze`.
- `blocked?` now returns true on a blocked HTTP status OR a body pattern match, so a 403/429 with unfamiliar copy is still detected, not just text the regex happens to know.

### 2. Fetcher retains the body on blocked (403/429) responses
`app/services/ingestion/fetcher.rb`
- Moved `body = response.body.to_s` before the success-status check.
- On a non-success response, the body is now retained when `error_code == "blocked"` (403/429), via a new `retain_body_on_error?` helper. Non-blocked errors (500, etc.) still discard the body. The streaming cap (`MAX_BYTES` / `ResponseTooLarge`) already bounds the size, so retention is safe.

### 3. Job probes a blocked fetch before failing (closes the loop)
`app/jobs/ingest_reviews_job.rb`
- Previously `return ... unless fetch_result.success?` ran before the probe, so a live 403 was never inspected.
- On a blocked fetch with a retained body, the job now runs the probe and persists `raw_fetch_metadata.probe` (with `status: "blocked"`) and the `BLOCKED_WARNING`, then fails with `FETCH_BLOCKED_MESSAGE`.
- Extracted `persist_probe` / `blocked_with_body?` helpers to avoid duplicating the probe-and-write block used by both the success and blocked paths.

### Tests added/updated
- `fetcher_test.rb`: rewrote the 403 test to assert the body is retained (20 bytes, `html_bytes` set) and added a 429 case. The existing 500 test still asserts `body` is discarded, keeping that contrast.
- `trustpilot_probe_test.rb`: added a test that 403 and 429 (with a benign body carrying no block patterns) both classify as `blocked` / `fail`.
- `ingest_reviews_job_test.rb`: added `blocked_fetch_result(html)` helper and a hard-block test asserting a retained 403 WAF body gets probed (`probe.status == "blocked"`, `http_status == 403`), warned, and failed correctly.

### Verification
- Real Trustpilot 403 through the actual fetcher + probe: `error_code=blocked`, body retained (991 bytes), `probe.status=blocked`, title "Verifying Connection".
- `bin/rubocop` on the three app files: clean.
- Full suite: 127 runs, 787 assertions, 0 failures.

### Net behavior change
A hard block (403/429) is now classified with the same fidelity as a soft block (200 + WAF text): the ingestion run records the probe result and the block warning instead of just a bare "fetch failed: blocked". This makes the block type and metadata visible in the UI/logs and removes the gap between the test stubs and the live path.

content.text:
No scraping library. I added nothing, I only edited the existing code. Here's what the codebase actually uses:

**Fetching: Ruby standard library only**
- `app/services/ingestion/fetcher.rb` uses `net/http` + `timeout` (both stdlib). A plain `Net::HTTP::Get` with a hardcoded `User-Agent: ReviewLensAI/1.0`. No gem.

**Parsing: Nokogiri**
- `Nokogiri::HTML` in `trustpilot_adapter.rb` and `trustpilot_probe.rb` to parse the fetched HTML.

So: **stdlib `Net::HTTP` for the request, Nokogiri for HTML parsing.** Nothing like Mechanize, HTTParty, or a headless browser.

Two things in the Gemfile that are easy to misread but are *not* used for scraping:
- `faraday` appears in `Gemfile.lock` but only as a transitive dependency (it backs RubyLLM's OpenAI calls in `app/services/ai/`). The fetcher does not use it.
- `capybara` + `selenium-webdriver` are in the Gemfile but are **test-only** (system tests), never used for ingestion.

This is directly relevant to why you're getting blocked: `Net::HTTP` with a static User-Agent and no JavaScript execution is trivially fingerprinted by AWS WAF Bot Control, which is exactly the 403 interstitial we captured. Any client that cannot run the `edge.sdk.awswaf.com` challenge JS will be blocked at the edge regardless of which HTTP library drives the request, which is why the honest conclusion (and the PR's stance) is to classify the block and fall back to manual import rather than swap HTTP libraries.

urls.title: Web Scraping with Ruby
urls.content:
In this tutorial we're diving into the world of web scraping with Ruby. We'll explore powerful Gems like Faraday for HTTP requests, Nokogiri for parsing HTML, and browser automation with Selenium and Capybara. Along the way, we'll scrape real websites with some example scripts, handle dynamic Javascript content and even run headless browsers in parallel. By the end of this tutorial, you'll be equipped with the knowledge and practical patterns needed to start scraping data from websites — whether for fun, research, or building something cool. ## Ruby Web Scraping Technology If you're new to Web Scraping with Ruby, here's a list of some of the most notable Gems that might be of use to you in your data extraction journey: * [faraday](https://github.com/lostisland/faraday) – Adapter-based HTTP client with middleware support. Great for building API clients. * [Capybara](https://github.com/teamcapybara/capybara) - Lets you control a real browser (like Chrome) using Selenium. * [httparty](https://github.com/jnunemaker/httparty) – Makes HTTP feel like a friendly Ruby object. Great for quick and readable requests. * [spidr](https://github.com/postmodern/spidr) – Flexible crawler that can work across domains or go deep on one. * [nokogiri](https://github.com/sparklemotion/nokogiri) – The gold standard. CSS selectors, XPath, all the good stuff. * [loofah](https://github.com/flavorjones/loofah) – Built on Nokogiri, focused on sanitizing and cleaning HTML. * [watir](https://github.com/watir/watir) – Built on Selenium. Super clean API for browser automation. * [mechanize](https://github.com/sparklemotion/mechanize) – Classic tool for navigating and interacting with pages. Knows about forms, links, cookies, etc. * [metainspector](https://github.com/jaimeiniesta/metainspector) – Pulls titles, descriptions, images, links, etc. * [addressable](https://github.com/sporkmonger/addressable) – Drop-in replacement for URI with full RFC compliance and template support. * [parallel](https://github.com/grosser/parallel) – Easy multithreading/multiprocessing. Super practical. * [oj](https://github.com/ohler55/oj) – Optimized JSON parser/generator, crazy fast. * [selenium](https://github.com/seleniumhq/selenium) – Drives real browsers. Chrome, Firefox, you name it. Now on with the Ruby tutorial. ## Prerequisites ### Expected knowledge and environment Before we dive in, here's what you'll need to follow along: * Basic knowledge of Ruby (you should know how to run scripts and install gems) * [Ruby 3.x](https://www.ruby-lang.org/en/downloads/) installed on your system * Any code editor (VS Code, Sublime, whatever makes you happy) * A terminal (bash, zsh, fish, the usual suspects) We're keeping it simple — no Rails, no heavy setup. Just clean and nice Ruby. ### Setting up your Ruby scraping project Let's create a new Ruby project folder real quick. Open your terminal and run: ``` mkdir ruby_scraper cd ruby_scraper bundle init ``` This will create a `Gemfile` for you. Now let's create the main Ruby script: ``` touch scraper.rb ``` You should now have: ``` ruby_scraper/ ├── Gemfile └── scraper.rb ``` We'll start adding gems and writing code in the next sections. ## Making HTTP requests with Ruby There are a _lot_ of ways to make HTTP requests in Ruby. Seriously — the Ruby ecosystem has more HTTP clients than I have unread emails. Well, in fact strong community is a big advantage of the language. You've got `Net::HTTP`, `HTTP.rb`, `HTTParty`, `Typhoeus`, `RestClient`, and probably a few options I've blocked from memory. But today we'll be utilizing [**Faraday**](https://lostisland.github.io/faraday/#/). It's flexible, pluggable, and easy to use. I like Faraday as some people like their morning coffee — reliable, configurable, and slightly addictive. No, actually I like it much more. Anyway... ### Getting started with Faraday First, add Faraday to your `Gemfile`. We'll go with version 2: ``` gem 'faraday', '~> 2.0' ``` Then install it: ``` bundle install ``` Let's make our first HTTP GET request to a sample web page. Open up `scraper.rb` and try this: ``` require 'faraday' response = Faraday.get('https://example.com') puts "Status: #{response.status}" puts "Headers: #{response.headers.inspect}" puts "Body: #{response.body[0..200]}..." # Show only the first 200 chars ``` In this example we're reading response status code, headers, and body. Run it with: ``` bundle exec ruby scraper.rb ``` You should see something like: ``` Status: 200 Headers: { ... } Body: ... ``` Congrats — you just scraped your first page. You're basically a hacker now! ### Customizing requests with Faraday Faraday's not just for basic GET requests — it's highly customizable. content.text:
Read it. Honest assessment of what's actually relevant to our Trustpilot block, since most of the article won't help us.

## What the article covers

1. **Faraday** (HTTP client with gzip + `faraday-retry` middleware) + **Nokogiri** for static-HTML scraping.
2. **Selenium + Capybara** driving a real headless Firefox/Chrome for JS-rendered pages and interaction.
3. **ScrapingBee** (the author's own paid API) for anti-bot bypass: premium proxies, rotation, JS rendering.
4. Niceties: CSV output, screenshots, retries, headless mode, cookie-popups.

## Verdict: almost none of it solves our block

This matters because the easy reading of the article ("switch to Faraday, add gzip, you're a scraper now") would not change our 403 outcome. Here's the honest split:

**Won't fix the AWS WAF 403:**
- **Faraday.** It uses `net-http` under the hood (`faraday-net_http`). Same fingerprint surface as our current `Net::HTTP`. The gzip/retry middleware is nice ergonomics, not anti-bot evasion. Swapping to it = cosmetic refactor, same block.
- **HTTParty / Mechanize / Typhoeus.** Same story. AWS WAF Bot Control does not care which Ruby HTTP library you use; it cares that you don't execute `edge.sdk.awswaf.com/.../challenge.js`. No plain HTTP client does.
- **Better headers / rotating a static User-Agent.** The block is a JS challenge, not a header heuristic. We'd still get the interstitial.

**Would actually bypass it:**
- **Selenium/Capybara headless browser.** A real Chrome/Firefox executes the WAF challenge JS, gets the cookie, and renders the real page. This is the only self-hosted option that works. Cost: heavy (headless Chrome in Docker, ~hundreds of MB RAM per worker), fragile (challenge JS can change), and it directly violates the spirit of your own AGENTS.md rule ("if blocked, stop, do not fight it" is stated for G2 but the reasoning applies equally to Trustpilot WAF).
- **A rendering/anti-bot proxy API (ScrapingBee, Bright Data, Zyte, etc.).** Outsourced JS rendering + residential proxy rotation. Works, but adds a paid third-party dependency and a secret, which conflicts with the "boring, no scope creep" PRD stance and the hiring-exercise framing.

## What this means for ReviewLens

Our current stack is already the article's recommended static stack: **stdlib `Net::HTTP` + Nokogiri.** We're not missing a library. The block is an anti-bot wall, and the article's own honest answer for anti-bot is "use a paid proxy service" (its section 5).

Three legitimate paths, in order of fit with your PRD:

1. **Stay the course (recommended).** Keep accurate block detection (PR #41 does this) and use the `manual_adapter` fallback. This is exactly what AGENTS.md prescribes for blocked platforms. Cheapest, most honest, ships today.
2. **Headless browser adapter.** Add a `Selenium/Capybara`-based fetch path behind a flag for blocked responses only. Real feasibility: would need Chrome in the Docker image, a worker-safe browser pool, and acceptance that Trustpilot can break it any time. For a hiring exercise, this is scope creep the PRD warns against.
3. **Paid scraping API.** Fastest to actually unblock, but adds a vendor secret + cost and a dependency the PRD's "no scope creep" rule pushes back on. I'd only do this if you have a demo-critical product you must show live ingestion for.

If you want, I can prototype option 2 behind a feature flag (headless fetch only when the probe reports `blocked`), but my recommendation is option 1 and to spend the effort on the manual-import UX instead. Which direction do you want?
What service is recommended?

messages.content: Compare current (2026) pricing and capabilities of web scraping APIs that handle JavaScript rendering and anti-bot bypass for sites protected by AWS WAF / CloudFront bot control. Focus on: ScrapingBee, Bright Data, ScrapingDog, ScraperAPI, ZenRows, Oxylabs. For each: per-1000-request credit cost, whether they offer residential proxies, JS rendering, and whether they successfully bypass AWS WAF bot challenges. Which is best value for a small project / demo with light usage?

content.text:
The six providers you listed all support JavaScript rendering and some level of anti‑bot handling, but only a subset publicly emphasize strong performance on modern WAF/bot systems; pricing is highly tiered and often jumps when you enable JS and premium proxies. For a *small, light‑usage demo*, **ScrapingBee** and **ZenRows** give the best balance of low entry cost, JS support, and simple plans; for serious AWS WAF/CloudFront bypass, **Oxylabs** and **Bright Data** are generally stronger but more expensive.

Below I’ll go provider by provider, then give a value recommendation.

---

## Important caveats

- Public docs and pricing pages rarely say explicitly “we bypass AWS WAF bot control”; claims are usually framed as “bypass Cloudflare/advanced anti‑bot.” Where AWS WAF performance is not benchmarked, I’ll call that out explicitly.
- Prices and credit weights for JS/premium proxies change frequently and differ by plan. I’ll use current public/summary data from 2025–2026 comparison articles and vendor docs, but for an actual project you should confirm on each vendor’s pricing page.
- “Per‑1000‑request cost” below is **entry‑tier effective price** for a simple HTML request; JS rendering or premium proxies often cost *multiple credits per request* and increase the effective per‑1000 cost.

---

## ScrapingBee

**Pricing (entry tier, 1000 requests)**  
- Entry plan: **$49/month for 50,000 requests ≈ $0.98 per 1,000 requests**[2].  
- That is for normal HTML requests; when you enable JS rendering or premium proxies, ScrapingBee applies **credit multipliers** (similar to other credit‑based APIs), so the *effective* cost per 1,000 JS + residential requests is higher.[3]  

**Residential proxies**  
- ScrapingBee markets “premium proxies” and geo‑targeting; these are generally residential/ISP‑grade IPs.[3]  
- Residential coverage is broad; exact pool composition isn’t publicly broken down, but it is positioned as suitable for hard targets (Cloudflare, major e‑commerce).[2][3]

**JavaScript rendering**  
- JS rendering is a core feature; included on base plans, with credit weighting per request.[3]  
- ScrapingBee measured a **97.05% success rate** in Proxyway’s 2025 benchmark for Amazon‑like, protected sites.[2]

**AWS WAF / CloudFront bot control bypass**  
- Benchmarks and marketing focus mostly on **Cloudflare** and Amazon; ScrapingBee is described as a “mid‑market” solution with solid unblocking against advanced anti‑bot systems.[3]  
- There is no public, AWS‑specific success number. Given the stack (browser‑based rendering + residential proxies + fingerprint management), ScrapingBee will generally work on many AWS WAF configurations, but *aggressive* WAF/bot control setups may show lower success than top‑tier providers like Oxylabs/Bright Data.[3]

**Summary for small/demo use**  
- Good **developer UX** and strong success rate on typical protected targets.[2][3]  
- Effective cost for a light demo with JS is moderate; not the cheapest, but predictable and widely used.

---

## Bright Data (Web Scraper API)

**Pricing (entry tier, 1000 requests)**  
- Web Scraper API “starts at **$0.75 per 1,000 successful requests** with no monthly commitment” for easy targets[3].  
- When you enable JS rendering or premium/residential proxies, Bright Data uses credit multipliers; their own 2026 comparison notes that **JS rendering and premium proxies can push cost “5 to 75×” higher per request on some providers**.[3] Bright Data’s multipliers are significant but clearly documented.

**Residential proxies**  
- Bright Data is one of the largest **residential proxy** networks; residential, mobile, and ISP proxies are a core product line.[3]  
- The Web Scraper API can be configured to use residential/mobile IPs for difficult targets, including anti‑bot/WAF‑protected sites.[3]

**JavaScript rendering**  
- JS rendering is integrated into the Web Scraper API; the Hobby‑style plan mentioned in Bright Data’s own comparison (“$29/month for 250,000 requests”) is for another vendor, but in their ranking article Bright Data confirms full JS support in their own API.[3]

**AWS WAF / CloudFront bot control bypass**  
- Bright Data explicitly highlights bypass capability for **Cloudflare and other bot systems** and is reported to consistently bypass Cloudflare in cited benchmarks.[3]  
- While AWS WAF isn’t named, the combination of residential/mobile proxies + browser fingerprint management typically works on AWS WAF/CloudFront bot control unless a site has unusual, custom behavior detection. In practice, Bright Data sits in the “top tier” for bypass strength.[3]

**Summary for small/demo use**  
- Technically strong, but **pricing is complex** and can become expensive once you mix JS rendering + residential proxies.  
- For a small demo, pay‑per‑success at $0.75/1,000 looks cheap, but expect much higher effective cost on tougher AWS‑protected endpoints.

---

## ScrapingDog

**Pricing (entry tier, 1000 requests)**  
- A 2026 Amazon scraping comparison cites **ScrapingDog’s Pro plan at ~$0.20 per 1,000 requests** (vendor pricing)[2].  
- That’s unusually low; as with others, this is for simple HTML requests. When you enable JS or advanced proxies, the cost per 1,000 goes up (via multipliers or higher plans).[2][3]

**Residential proxies**  
- ScrapingDog offers proxy rotation and geo‑targeting; some plans include access to residential‑grade IPs for difficult sites. Exact composition is not clearly detailed in public docs; they are positioned more as a cost‑efficient mid‑tier than as a premium unblocking provider.[2]

**JavaScript rendering**  
- ScrapingDog supports **headless browser/JS rendering** in its API (used in Amazon and general scraping guides), though this may consume more credits or require higher tiers.[2].

**AWS WAF / CloudFront bot control bypass**  
- Public material and benchmarks focus on Amazon, generic e‑commerce, and Cloudflare; there are no AWS‑specific success metrics.[2][3]  
- At this price point and positioning, ScrapingDog is best viewed as an **economical option for moderately protected sites**, not as a specialist for hard AWS WAF bot challenges.

**Summary for small/demo use**  
- Very low entry‑tier **price per 1,000** for basic HTML, but limited data on performance against strict AWS WAF/bot setups.  
- Good choice for a cheap demo where occasional failures are acceptable and the target’s AWS WAF is not aggressively tuned.

---

## ScraperAPI

**Pricing (entry tier, 1000 requests)**  
- Bright Data’s 2026 comparison notes that **ScraperAPI “starts at $49/month” alongside ScrapingBee and Oxylabs**[3].  
- $49/month with 100,000 requests would be ~$0.49 per 1,000; with 50,000, ~$0.98 per 1,000. Their actual current matrix is tiered; JS/premium proxies increase effective cost.

**Residential proxies**  
- ScraperAPI emphasizes automatic proxy rotation and multiple proxy types; like similar mid‑market providers, they offer residential proxies on higher plans for hard targets and geo‑sensitive scraping.[3]

**JavaScript rendering**  
- ScraperAPI supports JavaScript rendering via a “render” parameter or specific browser mode; this is an established feature in their API product line.[3]

**AWS WAF / CloudFront bot control bypass**  
- Public positioning is “bypass CAPTCHAs, IP bans, and anti‑bot protections” in general; independent benchmarks in the Bright Data article focus more on Cloudflare and e‑commerce than specifically AWS WAF.[3]  
- ScraperAPI is generally on par with ScrapingBee in the mid‑market: decent success on protected sites, but not the strongest on very strict WAF bot control.

**Summary for small/demo use**  
- Similar tier to ScrapingBee: mid‑range price, solid features, but not clearly optimized for the toughest AWS WAF configurations.  
- Reasonable option, but not clearly the best value vs ScrapingBee/ZenRows for a small JS‑heavy demo.

---

## ZenRows

**Pricing (entry tier, 1000 requests)**  
- The Bright Data comparison notes: **ZenRows starts at $69/month**[3]. Exact request quota for that plan is not quoted there; typical ZenRows entry tiers are in the tens of thousands of requests, so effective cost per 1,000 is usually around the $1 range for plain HTML.  
- As a credit‑based provider, **JS rendering and “Adaptive Stealth Mode”** consume more credits per request, increasing the effective per‑1,000 cost.[3][5].

**Residential proxies**  
- ZenRows explicitly highlights **residential proxies** as recommended for “medium or hard protection” targets and says its **Universal Scraper API “handles rotating proxies” for you**[5].  
- This is a clear signal that residential IPs are integrated into their managed API for difficult bot/WAF setups.

**JavaScript rendering**  
- ZenRows provides a **headless browser** and JS rendering as part of its Universal Scraper API; it also offers “Adaptive Stealth Mode” which bundles browser, fingerprint, and behavior simulation.[5].

**AWS WAF / CloudFront bot control bypass**  
- ZenRows positions its Universal Scraper API as “the most reliable solution for bypassing any anti‑bot measure, regardless of its complexity,” including Cloudflare, DataDome, PerimeterX, Akamai, etc.[5]  
- AWS WAF is not called out by name, but the described stack—rotating residential proxies + fortified headless browser + behavioral simulation—is exactly what is typically required for CloudFront bot control and advanced AWS WAF configurations[5].  
- Given that, ZenRows is plausibly quite strong on AWS WAF, though there is no independent AWS‑specific benchmark in the sources.

**Summary for small/demo use**  
- Strong technical stack (residential + stealth browser + behavior) for **hard anti‑bot/WAF** targets.[5]  
- Entry price is higher than ScrapingBee/ScrapingDog, but for a *small JS + WAF demo* the bundled “Adaptive Stealth Mode” can be good value because you don’t have to manually manage proxies/fingerprints.

---

## Oxylabs (Scraper API / Web Unblocker)

**Pricing (entry tier, 1000 requests)**  
- A 2026 Amazon scraping comparison states **Oxylabs starts at $49/month**, with effective costs ranging roughly from **$2.45 per 1,000 (Hobby) down to $0.475 per 1,000 (Enterprise)** depending on plan and volume[2].  
- Like others, JS rendering and use of premium/residential proxies can change credit consumption per request, raising the effective per‑1,000 price on tough targets.[3].

**Residential proxies**  
- Oxylabs is one of the largest **residential proxy** providers; residential, mobile, and datacenter pools are a core business.[3]  
- Their scraping/API products (Web Unblocker, etc.) allow you to route via residential/mobile IPs on hard anti‑bot/WAF targets.[3].

**JavaScript rendering**  
- Oxylabs’ higher‑tier products integrate **browser‑level JS rendering** and advanced TLS/fingerprint management (Web Unblocker); standard Scraper API is more request‑based but can be combined with JS in client code.

**AWS WAF / CloudFront bot control bypass**  
- In Bright Data’s ranking article, Oxylabs and Decodo are reported to match each other at **~85.88% success rate** in Proxyway’s 2025 benchmark for advanced Cloudflare and similar protections[3].  
- AWS WAF is not explicitly benchmarked, but Oxylabs is consistently listed among the **top unblocking providers** for Cloudflare and other modern anti‑bot systems[3]. That typically translates to strong performance on AWS WAF/CloudFront bot control as well.

**Summary for small/demo use**  
- Very strong **unblocking stack** with large residential pool, but entry‑tier pricing is higher than mid‑market competitors for equivalent volume.[2][3]  
- Best fit when AWS WAF bypass reliability is more important than cost; overkill for a very small demo unless you specifically want to evaluate top‑tier performance.

---

## AWS WAF / CloudFront bot control bypass – cross‑provider view

From the sources:

- **Top‑tier bypass (Cloudflare and comparable systems)**: Bright Data, Oxylabs, Decodo (Smartproxy’s API brand) consistently benchmark as leaders for Cloudflare and complex anti‑bot systems.[3]  
- **Mid‑market, good but not top**: ScrapingBee, ScraperAPI, ZenRows, ScrapingDog are positioned as mid‑market/mid‑price, with good success on Amazon and Cloudflare but less explicit emphasis on “hardest possible” WAF setups.[2][3][5].  
- **ZenRows** is unusual among mid‑market providers in explicitly describing a composite stack—residential proxies + fortified headless browser + behavioral simulation—that is tailored to advanced anti‑bot systems, including those similar in design to AWS WAF bot control.[5].

None of the vendor‑authored or comparison sources explicitly publish “success rate vs AWS WAF bot control,” but based on their architecture:

- Providers with **residential/mobile proxies + true browser fingerprinting + behavior simulation** (Bright Data, Oxylabs, ZenRows) are most likely to reliably pass AWS WAF/CloudFront bot checks at scale.[3][5].
- Providers that primarily rely on data‑center proxies and simpler JS rendering may work on default or lightly tuned AWS WAF but will see more challenges or blocks under stricter policies.

---

## Per‑1000‑request entry‑tier costs (approximate)

These figures are for **basic requests**, before JS/proxy multipliers; for JS + residential, expect a multiple of these numbers.

| Provider       | Entry plan (approx)                         | Approx cost per 1,000 basic requests |
|----------------|---------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| **ScrapingBee** | $49/month for 50,000 requests[2]           | **$0.98 / 1,000**                     |
| **Bright Data** | Web Scraper API from 0.75/1,000 success[3] | **$0.75 / 1,000** (easy targets)      |
| **ScrapingDog** | Pro plan ≈ 0.20/1,000[2]                   | **$0.20 / 1,000**                     |
| **ScraperAPI**  | Starts at $49/month[3]                     | **~$0.5–0.98 / 1,000** (plan‑dependent) |
| **ZenRows**     | Starts at $69/month[3]                     | **≈$1 / 1,000** (plan‑dependent)      |
| **Oxylabs**     | $2.45/1,000 (Hobby) → $0.475/1,000 (Ent.)[2] | **$2.45 down to $0.475 / 1,000**   |

Again, **JS rendering + residential proxies** often cost several credits per request. For example, one mid‑market API cited in Bright Data’s article has **JS at 5 credits, premium proxies at 10, both at 25 credits** per request, meaning the *effective* per‑1,000 price can be 5–25× the simple HTML cost once you turn everything on.[3]

---

## Best value for a small project/demo with light usage

Assuming:

- You need **JS rendering**.
- You will hit at least some **AWS WAF/CloudFront‑protected endpoints**, but this is a *demo* or low‑volume project where 5–10% failure is tolerable.
- You care about **cost and simplicity**, not just raw bypass power.

A practical ranking:

1. **ZenRows** – best “all‑in‑one” for a small anti‑bot demo  
   - Pros: Residential proxies, JS rendering, and “Adaptive Stealth Mode” (behavior + fingerprint) all **managed in one API**.[5]  
   - Likely to perform well on AWS WAF/CloudFront without manual tuning.  
   - Con: Entry price (~$69/month, ≈$1/1,000 before multipliers) is higher than ScrapingBee/ScrapingDog, but for *light usage* you’re paying for convenience and bypass reliability rather than raw volume.[3][5].

2. **ScrapingBee** – strong mid‑market option with good benchmarks  
   - Pros: Good developer UX, reputable success rate (~97% on Amazon‑like targets)[2], JS and premium proxies available.  
   - Con: Somewhat higher effective cost per JS+residential request once multipliers apply, and less explicit focus on hardest WAF/bot setups.[2][3].

3. **ScrapingDog** – cheapest basic requests, acceptable for non‑aggressive AWS WAF  
   - Pros: Very low entry cost (~$0.20 per 1,000 basic requests)[2].  
   - Con: Less documented performance on strict AWS WAF; good for a *budget‑constrained* demo where occasional failures are acceptable[2].

4. **ScraperAPI** – similar to ScrapingBee but with less benchmark data  
   - Pros: Familiar API, JS + proxies, mid‑range pricing[3].  
   - Con: No clear advantage over ScrapingBee/ZenRows on either cost or bypass sophistication.

5. **Bright Data** – technically excellent, but likely overkill for a small demo  
   - Pros: Large residential/mobile pools, strong Cloudflare/bot bypass benchmarks[3].  
   - Con: JS + residential multipliers can make per‑1,000 costs high on difficult targets; configuration is more complex than ZenRows/ScrapingBee for a quick demo[3].

6. **Oxylabs** – best when you specifically want top‑tier unblocking  
   - Pros: Very strong residential network and unblocking stack; Proxyway benchmark success nearly matches top competitors[3].  
   - Con: Higher entry‑tier cost (up to $2.45 per 1,000 basic requests on Hobby)[2]; this is usually not the best value for *light* demo usage unless you explicitly want to test top‑tier AWS WAF performance.

**If you want a single recommendation for a small, technical demo hitting AWS WAF/CloudFront:**

- Use **ZenRows** if your primary goal is “show that we can bypass modern bot/WAF controls with minimal integration work,” and you can afford ~$69 for a month of testing.[3][5]
- Use **ScrapingBee** if you want a widely used, mid‑market API with good tooling and you’re willing to accept that very aggressive AWS WAF setups might require tuning or another provider.[2][3]
- Use **ScrapingDog** if lowest cash cost matters more than reliability and the target’s AWS WAF configuration is not extremely strict.[2]

For a more rigorous choice, you’d ideally run a quick A/B test: 100–500 requests per provider against your specific AWS WAF/CloudFront target and measure success rate, then weigh that against each provider’s JS+residential effective cost per 1,000 requests.

## Verdict: ScrapingBee, if you proceed. But you probably shouldn't for this project.

Here's the grounded breakdown.

### If you want to ship a scraping service, use ScrapingBee

For a single blocked target at demo scale, **ScrapingBee** is the right pick, not the "strongest" bypass provider:

1. **Free tier.** 1,000 credits on signup. Enough to validate Trustpilot works without spending anything. ZenRows ($69/mo) and Oxylabs have no useful free tier for this.
2. **You already have the integration code.** The article you just sent ends with the exact Faraday snippet. Our `Fetcher` swaps to it in ~20 lines behind the `blocked` branch.
3. **Good enough for AWS WAF.** ~97% success on protected-Amazon benchmarks. Trustpilot's CloudFront/WAF config is not the hardest target. The "top tier" providers (Bright Data, Oxylabs) are better but cost more and add complexity you don't need for one URL.
4. **Simple secret model.** One `SCRAPINGBEE_API_KEY` in `.kamal/secrets`, matches your existing secrets pattern.

Runners up: **ZenRows** if you find ScrapingBee fails on Trustpilot's specific WAF (its "Adaptive Stealth Mode" is genuinely stronger on bot challenges, ~$69/mo entry). **ScrapingDog** if you just want the cheapest possible demo (~$0.20/1k base) and accept flakiness. Avoid Bright Data and Oxylabs, they're enterprise-scale and overkill.

### The honest push-back: this conflicts with your own PRD

Before you integrate anything, weigh this against what the exercise is actually grading. From `AGENTS.md`:

- "If G2 blocks, stop, do not fight it" (stated for G2, same logic applies to Trustpilot WAF)
- "The PRD explicitly warns against scope creep toward a generic data ingestion platform"
- The three grading criteria are: deployed product, grounded answers with guardrails, committed AI transcripts. **Bypassing WAF is not one of them.**

The assignment is testing whether you build a grounded Q&A with refusals, not whether you can defeat AWS WAF. GetApp is the primary platform; manual import is the sanctioned fallback. A reviewer who sees a paid scraping vendor with an API key in a hiring exercise could read it two ways, and one of them is "didn't read the constraints."

### What I'd actually do

1. **Keep PR #41** (accurate block detection + manual fallback). It satisfies the PRD honestly.
2. **Improve the manual-import UX** so the demo path is smooth without live scraping.
3. **Only add ScrapingBee** if a reviewer specifically asks "can you show live ingestion?" and GetApp also blocks. Behind a feature flag, off by default, with the free-tier key in `.kamal/secrets`. Treat it as a demo-day insurance policy, not a core feature.

Want me to prototype the ScrapingBee integration behind a flag (used only when the probe reports `blocked`, falling back to manual import on failure), or work on the manual-import UX instead?
